data.title: Playtika Is Undervalued With Dividend Strength And D2C Momentum
data.description:
I’m bullish on Playtika Holding Corp. (PLTK). The stock looks cheap. There’s a good chance margins keep improving thanks to more people buying straight from Playtika, not the app stores. Wall Street’s missing how much cash these games keep making and how the new titles might really move the needle. At about 6 times forward earnings and an enterprise value just over 5 times EBITDA, investors are acting like Playtika’s best days are gone. But the company still has solid growth, is paying out a nearly 11% dividend and is changing its mix for the better. If Playtika just hits its own goals for direct sales and manages to keep its main games from shrinking too fast, I see the stock going up 40 to 60% or more, with the high dividend as a safety net.

The market’s making two big mistakes. One, it assumes Slotomania’s drop means all their games are sinking. Other titles are holding up or even growing. Two, hardly anyone’s giving credit for the extra profit Playtika gets from direct sales. After a rough year (down 48%) and a few missed earnings, the bar’s low. If management can just deliver “okay” results, the upside is big.

Business Snapshot

Playtika is still one of the biggest names in mobile gaming, especially with games you play for free but can buy extras in. The company is moving away from its historical reliance on slot games and spreading out its bets with more casual hits like Bingo Blitz, June’s Journey, Solitaire Grand Harvest and now the SuperPlay lineup. Disney Solitaire is already a smash, running at a $200 million yearly pace. CEO Robert Antokol said, “Disney Solitaire continues to outperform expectations, establishing itself as one of 2025’s standout new mobile launches.” Playtika’s deal with Disney is getting bigger and new games are on the way.

The push for direct sales is paying off. Management put it plainly: “D2C revenue crossed the $200 million threshold to $209.3 million, up 19% sequentially and up 20% year-over-year... D2C represented 31% of total revenue this quarter, and we are working to achieve 40% on a run rate basis in the next 2 years.” These are real results, not just talk. Changes to app store rules are helping Playtika keep more money from each sale by opening new payment and distribution paths, which management says support higher D2C adoption and margins, though implementation and testing are still ongoing.

SuperPlay wasn’t cheap to buy. It’s already working out and is now the company’s main growth driver. The earnings call presentation shows that losses from slots are being covered by gains in Bingo Blitz ($162.6 million revenue last quarter, up 1.7% from a year ago) and June’s Journey (down about 2.7% year-over-year, but more money per player as direct sales grow). Playtika’s new live events and in-game offers keep players spending and sticking around, which should keep profits up.

Mobile gaming is crowded. The kind of games Playtika makes, social casino and casual, still get steady play and solid spending. People worry that ads and marketing are getting more expensive, app store policies could change, and old slot games might keep fading. But people are still spending plenty of money on mobile games. Playtika’s move to direct sales means they’re less at the mercy of Apple and Google taking a cut. Playtika’s long list of big games and new launches shows management isn’t standing still.

Financials

The latest quarter shows the story clearly. Revenue hit $674.6 million, up 8.7% from last year. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $217.5 million, up 10.3%. Net income was $39.1 million. That’s about flat from last year, but up nearly 18% from the prior quarter. Sales directly to players now make up 31% of revenue and are on track to hit management’s 40% run-rate target within roughly the next two years. Gross margin is a strong 72.5%, and operating margin is 14.9%. Operating cash flow is still healthy, with $281.8 million so far this year and $587.9 million in cash on hand. Debt is big ($2.38 billion long-term), but the company has increased fixed-rate exposure through interest-rate swaps and has only modest near-term maturities.

The huge drop in Slotomania (down 46.7% year-over-year) is no surprise. Management said they planned for it, calling it “the deliberate rebalancing of the game economy we initiated earlier this year… While we work through these changes, we intentionally reduced performance marketing to avoid inefficient spending.” The engine driving profit is now more spread out. Bingo Blitz, June’s Journey and Disney Solitaire are leading the charge. Dividends and buybacks are keeping shareholders happy and help put a floor under the stock.

At $3.7 a share, Playtika trades at just over 5.4 times EBITDA and about 6 times next year’s expected earnings. If you use consensus earnings for 2026 ($0.6 per share) and slap on a still-cautious 12x multiple, you get $7.20. That’s about 90% higher than where it trades now. Even at 10x earnings, it’s $6, which is more than 60% up from here. Use the industry average for EBITDA, and the price is still in the $5.40 to $6 range. The 11% dividend yield helps protect the downside. The stock is cheap because investors keep assuming slot revenue will keep falling fast, and the company won’t make it up elsewhere. The numbers don’t back that up.

Sentiment

Many investors have given up on Playtika, as shown by the 48% drop in the last year and how nobody really cared about the recent good news. News headlines are mostly negative or mixed. Some call results “mixed” but also point out Playtika is hitting its targets and launching new winners. Analysts have lowered earnings estimates again and again, so expectations are low. If Playtika just holds steady or even surprises a little on the upside, the stock could jump as sentiment catches up with reality.

Risks

The biggest worries are whether Playtika can keep shifting to direct sales and if old games like Slotomania keep shrinking. If the SuperPlay deal doesn’t pan out or new games flop, profits could take a hit. The company carries a lot of debt, but there’s enough cash to pay the bills and nothing big is due soon. If the company fails to get more sales directly from players or legacy games don’t find a bottom, earnings might fall 15 to 20%. Even then, dividends look safe, but share price gains would be limited.

Outlook

Over the next year or two, three things will matter: getting direct sales up to 40% of revenue, keeping non-slot games growing or at least steady and launching at least one new big hit (especially with Disney). Management sounds confident, and the numbers back them up: “Our direct-to-consumer mix continued to expand margins… D2C represented 31% of total revenue this quarter, and we are working to achieve 40% on a run rate basis in the next 2 years.” Wall Street thinks revenue will increase to $2.81 billion next year and $2.89 billion in 2027, with earnings rising too. I think they’re being too cautious. If Playtika beats just one of those targets, the stock will move.

If things go badly, Slotomania keeps shrinking, direct sales slow down and new games disappoint. Earnings could drop and the stock might drop to around $3 - $3.25, with not much upside but still a decent dividend. If things go as planned, direct sales reach 37 to 39%, main games stay strong, Disney Solitaire delivers. Earnings and cash flow rise, and the stock is worth $5.5 to $6.5. In the best case, direct sales hit 40%, slots find a floor and new titles take off. Earnings pop and the stock could be $7.5 to $8 or higher, more than doubling from here.

Final Take

Playtika is undervalued. The company is proving it can switch to a better business model, has real new growth drivers, and keeps pumping out cash for investors. If management even does an average job, the stock should bounce back sharply. The only way this doesn’t work is if the old games crash harder and direct sales don’t keep growing. I don’t see that happening based on what’s in the numbers. This is a buy for anyone willing to bet on the turnaround, take the dividend, and wait for the market to notice what’s really going on.

data.title: 2026 Forecast: Don't Fight The Fed Until It's Dead
data.description:
Since the first round of Quantitative Easing in 2008, the Fed has been medicating the stock market with loose monetary policies every time the faintest hint of a recession wafts by. This maneuver has simultaneously led to one of the biggest bull markets of all time and made our economy seemingly recession-free ever since. But it all smacks of that proverbial Faustian bargain—instant gain for long-term pain, which can be ignored in the interim.

The Fed has indicated it will continue to lower interest rates in 2026 to create liquidity, even as the stock market rushes from one historic high to the next.

The liquidity excuse evaporates under the bright lights of the facts, as shown below. But this may be a moot point as the Fed’s ability to bend long-term rates downward by lowering the Federal Funds rate is pretty much gone.

Fig. 1

Notice that the chart in Fig. 1 shows a rise in the benchmark 10-year Treasury when the Fed attempted to force the rate down in the fourth quarter of 2024 and 2025. A rinse and repeat of this strategy is likely to have the same results in 2026, which will translate to slightly higher mortgage, auto loan, and credit card rates, not lower.

The Fed And Treasury Dept. Quietly Ramp Up QE, But It’s Not QE It’s RMP

The Fed’s recent inability to lower the benchmark 10-year Treasury by lowering the Federal Funds rate may be why both the Fed and the Treasury Department have turned toward other tools. This past October (2025) the Fed announced it would end its Quantitative Tightening program and followed up two months later by announcing that it would mimic the Treasury Department’s policy of buying back US Treasuries, for the sake of creating liquidity for the banking industry. But this is not quantitative easing, according to Fed officials. Rather this strategy is known as Reserve Management Purchases or RMPs.

Be that as it may, any suggestion that the Fed has completed its job of tightening up its balance sheet is hardly credible. Before the Fed initiated Quantitative Easing in 2008, it carried between $870 billion to $900 billion in assets on its balance sheet. The Fed’s assets—in the form Treasuries, corporate bonds, and mortgage-backed securities—rose 10-fold to $9 trillion dollars over the course of four rounds of QE.

The Fed launched its Quantitative Tightening program in 2022, and over the next 3 years managed to mop up roughly $2 trillion or so of the $9 trillion on its balance sheet. But that still leaves it with a balance of $7 trillion, which is still 777% higher than where it stood at $900 billion in early 2008.

Fig. 2

Adding further force to the government’s liquidity pump, the Treasury Department has signaled it will double down on its bond buyback program in 2026, increasing its purchases of 10-year, 20-year, and 30-year bonds from two times per quarter to four times per quarter.

So while the stock market soars from one record high to the next, it all begs the question—are there consequences for artificially generating loads of fuel for an obviously overheated market? Much like an over-zealous US Forest Service rushing to put out every minor brush fire as dry kindling builds up on the forest floor (they don’t do that anymore BTW, the US Forest Service that is) so the Fed and Treasury Department have outdone themselves in preventing the next recession for going on 18 years now, an astonishing record insofar recessions occurred every eight years or so on average, up until the Great Recession of 2008-09.

Who Exactly Needs All Of This Liquidity?

Getting right to the point, nobody needs this excess liquidity, certainly not the stock market as the Shiller PE ratio rises to historic highs, indicating Wall Street is severely overheated.

Neither do the banks need this excess liquidity, according to the Federal Reserve Bank’s own reports. The Fed reported just last month that our nation’s banks are currently maintaining capital well above requirements.

In October of 2025, the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas published a report claiming “Ample liquidity for a safe and efficient banking system”. Apparently, providing further liquidity for the banking system is completely unnecessary according to the Fed’s own reports.

The Real Reason For Bond Buybacks And Rate Reductions: The Government’s Soaring Interest Payments

Once we clear away the flimsy excuses, one obvious reason for this program of rate reductions and bond buybacks becomes clear. The US government’s monthly interest payments on its soaring debt is, predictably, also soaring.

Fig. 3

But if the Fed and Treasury Department stated openly that they were lowering the Federal Funds rate and buying back Treasuries at an accelerating rate in their desperation to hold down the government’s biggest expense—interest payments on our soaring debt—this shocking admission wouldn’t just be politically radioactive, it might send the stock market into a tailspin. The graceful way out for the Fed and Treasury Department is to pretend like they are taking responsible measures (liquidity) rather than desperate ones (holding back a massive debt bomb).

Once the next real recession comes, the question is how easily can the Fed turn it around by employing the usual measures when the usual measures are already being employed in a policy of kicking the can down the road and praying for a miracle?

This Time It Really Is Different

One big difference between pre-Great Recession financial policy and post-Great Recession financial policy is the amount of liquidity being artificially generated by the Fed and the Treasury Department as a type of new normal. Since 2008, the Fed has spent 137 months on its quantitative easing program, but just 55 months on quantitative tightening. In other words, it has spent well over twice as much time easing as opposed to tightening, and even though its balance sheet is still running hot at $7 trillion, the Fed—along with its partner the Treasury Department—has returned to loose-money policies.

This incessant and excessive market easing—whether by lowering the Federal Funds rate, or buying back Treasuries on the open market, or purchasing mortgage-backed securities at a price generously above market value—appears to be open-ended with no plan in sight to return the Fed’s balance sheet to anything resembling normal.

The Biggest Black Swan On The Horizon: The Bear Steepener

One trend that could quickly unravel the markets is a bear steepener.

Even though the Fed’s rate-lowering policy has resulted in higher rates in long-term Treasuries, it still has the effect of lowering rates on short-term treasuries as you can see in the chart below.

Fig. 4

But this is cold comfort once you realize that further drives apart the 10-year and 3-month Treasuries in a trend known as a ‘bear steepener’, and that is historically the most accurate sign of a looming recession.

In Fig. 5 below, the red ovals show that when the 10 year Treasury rate dips below the 3-month Treasury rate, and then rises above it again, in virtually every case, a recession follows.

Fig. 5

The last real recession began December 2007. Once the Fed engaged in four rounds of QE (and appears to be currently engaged in a fifth) there hasn’t been a recession since. While the Fed chart above categorizes the four-week market correction in 2020 as a recession, this simply isn’t credible considering that economists define a recession as 2 fiscal quarters (6 months) of negative GDP growth. It appears the Fed may have included the needle-thin “recession” on the far right as a financial fig leaf to cover up the fact that they have been all too thorough in their efforts to avoid naturally occurring recessions by over-medicating the markets.

Be that as it may, the Treasury market is currently in a bear steepening formation. Virtually every other time this formation has occurred, it has been soon followed by a recession. Except in the case of 2020, when it was waved away by exploding the M2 money supply.

Fig. 6

But now the question is, will the Fed have enough fuel to explode the M2 money supply once again, without triggering runaway inflation, which can come on all too quickly, as we witnessed in 2022 when inflation peaked at 9.1% that June.

Attempting this maneuver to wave away another oncoming recession while aggregate debt exceeds historic highs at an accelerating rate is risky to say the least.

How Does It All End?

Determining the exact moment that the Fed and Treasury Department run out of the financial fuel they need to artificially hold bond rates down is a difficult call for even the best analysts. JP Morgan’s famous CEO, Jamie Dimon, predicted a 33% market correction back in April 2022. He recently repeated his prediction of a market downturn this past October, though he softened his stance, suggesting markets could correct 20% sometime in the next 6 to 48 months.

Since 2023, at Warren Buffet’s direction, Berkshire Hathaway has been steadily liquidating equities into cash. As of this Janurary the investment goliathon accumulated a mountainous $382 billion dollars in cash and short-term Treasuries in anticipation of some sort of upcoming bargain hunt.

Keep Watching the 10-year Treasury

Of all the twists of fortune that could manifest in the coming year, the one most likely to trigger a dramatic market reversal is a sudden and unstoppable rise in long-term Treasury rates, particularly the benchmark 10-year Treasury.

If it breaks out higher and the Fed loses its ability to rein it in, the entire market economic model, which appears to be based on QE to Eternity, will quickly unravel.

Fig. 7

Fig. 8

Other Black Swans Darkening The Landscape

Margin debt at historic highs

The margin debt—created when investors borrow money from their trading platforms to buy equities—has soared to historic highs, exceeding $1.2 trillion by the end of 2025. This is significant because margin debt tends to soar just before a sharp market reversal.

Consumer Debt At All-time Highs

US consumer debt reached an all-time high of $18.59 trillion as of the 3rd quarter of 2025. This includes mortgages, auto loans, credit cards, and student loans. College debt delinquencies (missing payments for over 90 days) have risen to 10%.

Circular Financing in the AI Sector

Circular financing is a system wherein a corporation offers to invest a large sum of money in another corporation in order to allow the recipient enough financing to buy products from the company making the loan. This allows the company making the loan to claim that they are selling a large volume of product, even though they have gone cash negative on the transaction rather than cash positive. The effect of these risky transactions is to create a financial mirage that can fool other investors into thinking that a company is far more profitable than it actually is. If enough investors buy into this false impression of profitability, a bubble can form and then easily pop once the financial realities catch up. The argument that AI is not in a bubble because AI is here to stay falls flat once we acknowledge that the World Wide Web is also here to stay, but that did not stop the Dot.com bubble from popping in 2001.

Short Term Forecast for 2026: Sunny Weather With Strong Tailwinds

The extra liquidity that the Fed and Treasury are planning on pumping into the markets signals that investors can keep riding the stock market wave for the first two, possibly three quarters ahead. Investors who choose to stay in the market would be well advised to add stop-loss orders to their stock trades. This is the sort of trading environment that absolutely requires a safety net.

Longterm Forecast: Prepare For the 100-Year Storm

If history is any guide, the massive amount of debt building up in the economy will result in a steep market correction. If the next recession doesn’t come in the final quarter of this year, it will likely come in the final quarter of 2027. Whether this year or next, it will eventually come as both the Fed and the Treasury Department burn through the last of their economic kindling.

Summary

The liquidity that both the Fed and the Treasury Department are pumping into the economy will likely have its usual effect of raising all boats in the stock market for the short term. But the accelerating and unaddressed historic super-debt building up in the system could burst the bubble at any moment. The best indicator to watch is a likely breakout in the 10-year Treasury. However, the 10-year has been sending a breakout signal for the past 3 quarters, so there’s no telling how long this opera diva of an indicator can hold that high-C. The best strategy ahead is to be like Warren Buffet and put a decent portion of your stock portfolio in cash and/or short-term Treasuries to have dry kindling when the big correction comes. And place stop-loss orders on your equity investments.

data.title: Gold Market Commentary: Precious Metal Thunder
data.description:
Highlights

December and FY 2025 review

December’s 4% gain took gold’s full-year return to a multi-decade record of 67% y/y.

Central banks, tariff and trade risks, strong options market activity and a weaker US dollar all played a part.

Looking forward

The December precious metals surge, including silver and platinum, and commodity index rebalancing could elicit some near-term volatility. But beyond short-term effects, gold will likely hum to its own tune.

Upcoming tariff rulings by the US Supreme Court will likely have important implications for US trade policy. The effect on gold may be more nuanced but potentially supportive.

Finally, the continued stream of geopolitical flareups, most recently exemplified with US actions in Venezuela, creates support for gold and highlights that it has become the safe haven of choice.

Gold pushed to “eleven”

Performance

Gold tagged its 53rd all-time PM price high for the year, reaching US$4,449/oz on 23 December, before closing the year at US$4,368/oz (Chart 1). It was a stellar finish to a stellar year – posting a December return of 4.2% to take the full year return to 67%. All-time-highs were set in all the major currencies, but with some annual variation in the final return tally due to FX volatility (Table 1).

Attribution

In December, our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) suggested that options activity - a component of risk & uncertainty - was a major contributor to the monthly return, followed by EM FX where US dollar weakening vs the Chinese renminbi (+1.2%) was a key factor. (Chart 2).

For 2025 as a whole, the explicit variables in our model explained c.60% of the FY return, led by geopolitical risk and options market activity, particularly from August to October. A weaker US dollar was also instrumental, led by EM FX. Falling yields provided a slightly smaller but still important boost. See Table 1 on our Gold Outlook 2026 for further details.

Chart 1: Gold has reached 95 new ATHs since 2024

All-time highs in the LBMA PM benchmark price (US$/oz)*

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data as of 6 January 2025.

Table 1: Stable FX ensured similar gold price strength across currencies in December

Prices and returns in various currencies

*As of 31 December 2025. Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD, expressed in local currencies, except for India and China where the MCX Gold Price PM and Shanghai Gold Benchmark PM are used, respectively.

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

On the flipside, the cumulative lagged gold monthly return was a drag on the full year. This reflects how effectively the gold market self-corrects and balances – even in a bull market – via gold’s dual nature channels of consumer and investor demand. It also contributes to gold’s low average volatility among the commodity complex.

The full-year residual contribution likely reflected some central bank, tariff-related or Commodity Trading Advisor (CTA)/retail activity that the explicit variables did not. In addition, a higher constant - which captures the unconditional mean return after accounting for all explicit factors – likely reflected the persistent demand impact from central banks during the year. We see this as a feature, not a flaw.

Gold ETFs and futures

In December, global gold ETFs managed a seventh consecutive month of inflows, dominated by North American funds, as they had been for the full year. Futures positioning data had caught up by year end showing an increase in managed money net longs of US$11bn (59t) for December, an increase in value of US$8bn but a fall in tonnage by 173t for the full year.

Chart 2: Strong options activity and a weaker US dollar helped drive gold higher in December

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 31 December 2025. Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) is a multiple regression model of monthly gold price returns, which we group into four key thematic driver categories of gold’s performance: economic expansion, risk & uncertainty, opportunity cost, and momentum. These themes capture motives behind gold demand; most importantly, investment demand, which is considered the marginal driver of gold price returns in the short run. The ‘residual’ represents the percentage change in the gold price that is not explained by factors already included. Results shown here are based on analysis covering a five-year estimation period using monthly data. Alternative estimation periods and data frequencies are available on Goldhub.com.

Precious metal thunder

  • December’s surge in precious metals was historic in scale but uneven in nature, with silver and platinum exhibiting clear signs of a policy-driven squeeze, in contrast to gold’s relatively more measured ascent.
  • Near-term risks from silver and platinum volatility may briefly weigh on gold, but the medium-term outlook for all three metals remains constructive, limiting the likelihood of a sustained drag.
  • January poses some additional minor event risks for gold, including index rebalancing and a possible Supreme Court ruling on IEEPA tariffs. We see the outcomes here as mostly positive for gold.
  • The US capture of Maduro and ongoing tensions in Venezuela reminds us that geopolitical flare-ups tend to recede quite quickly, but the frequency of such events and their cumulative effect over the last year has likely raised the pulse rate of markets and risk premia with it. Gold seems to be the main beneficiary in these scenarios.

December’s thundering surge across precious metals helped deliver a y/y return for the sector not seen in 45 years (Chart 3) and (Chart 4).

The final leg higher appeared less about an acceleration of structural tailwinds - such as real-asset demand, fiscal concerns or dollar weakness - and more about a policy, and near-market supply squeeze.

Chart 3: Thundering into year end

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 31 December 2025. All prices US$/oz.

Chart 4: Party like it’s 1979…almost

Annual return of precious metals*

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data from 1975 to 2025. Due to data availability, returns from 1975 to 1995 are based on the S&P GSCI Precious Metals Total Return Index (which includes gold and silver only), while returns from 1996 to 2025 use the S&P GSCI Precious Metals, Platinum & Palladium Equal Weighted TR Index.

Tariff uncertainty and trade frictions pulled forward investor purchases of physical supply, steepened backwardation (Chart 5), and inflated regional premia (Chart 6), most visible in silver and platinum. China’s announcement on 27 December of export licenses for silver, added fuel to the fire.1

Chart 5: Tenuous signs of tightness, except for gold

Precious metals 12-month swap rates*

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 31 December 2025. Swap rate reflects spread between forward price and spot price. A negative value suggests backwardation, or demand for immediate supply. Volume in future contracts subjects the data to a level of unreliability.

Chart 6: China policy on silver a major driver

Chinese local price premium/discount to global price*

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 31 December 2025. Each series compared the local spot price in China, converted to US$/oz to the global spot price for the metal and then displayed in relative % terms.

Gold continued to move higher in December, but unlike the other precious metals, lacked the hallmarks of a squeeze. This divergence became clear when the seemingly inevitable reversal began late in the month, triggered by raised margin requirements at the CME. The resulting de-risking of leveraged positions, in the context of thin liquidity and year-end tax effects, drove intraday ranges in silver, platinum and palladium to over five times their 2025 average. Gold’s range, by contrast, was just three times its average – a sign of both deeper liquidity and a fundamentally different set of drivers.

While the structural support for gold remains intact, there are near-term channels through which weakness in silver and platinum could briefly propagate: cooling of the ‘scarcity trade’, some rotation into base metals, early-January index rebalancing (post 8 January), and a potential uptick in recycling – particularly in silver, where above-ground stocks are more readily mobilised. There is a small risk this feeds back to gold, which so far has staved off a material rise in price-driven recycling.

That said, the medium-term backdrop for silver – and to a lesser extent platinum – remains constructive, limiting the risk that recent volatility becomes more than a short-term drag on gold’s own story.

Beyond the noise, the signal should emerge

Gold’s support is less dependent on tight physical markets and more on its macro role: sustained central bank demand, hedging demand in a world of policy uncertainty, and diversification demand when stock-bond correlations remain elevated.

Policy could again be the near-term catalyst. The Supreme Court heard challenges to IEEPA-based tariffs in November, with a decision expected in early 2026. A ruling that validates tariffs, barring some short-term relief, would reinforce policy risk and the case for hedging; one that constrains them could compress the policy premium. But tariffs have also provided a partial buffer to the fiscal outlook – and if that buffer is removed, attention is likely to shift back toward deficits and term premia. The effect may be more nuanced for gold, but we believe either outcome could be supportive.

There is also a risk of repricing the Fed’s policy rate path, following the unexpectedly strong US Q3 GDP print.

Historically, cumulative Fed cuts of 175bp or more have coincided with falling bond yields; the late-1990s were the notable exception. Today’s parallel lies in optimistic AI-driven growth expectations but with inflation expectations considerably higher. While firmer rate expectations could temper near-term enthusiasm for gold from certain investors, the inflationary implications should remain supportive over the medium term (Chart 7).

Finally, it is too soon to say what direct and indirect implications of the ongoing tensions in Venezuela and the region will be. Suffice to say that the immediate reaction by assets normally sought during geopolitical spikes is telling. Prior to 2022 – a date selected because it represents both the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the year when the return of inflation was at its worst – the US dollar was on average the main beneficiary of geopolitical spikes. Judging by gold’s reaction this week, it now appears to be the haven of choice.

Chart 7: It is unusual for the 10-year Treasury yield to rise after so many policy rate cuts

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 31 December 2025. Boxes show the change in the Fed Funds rate (FFR) and 10-year Treasury yield in % points from the start of the cutting cycle to at least 175bps of FFR rate cuts, where that occurred. Where the cutting cycle was milder, the maximum FFR drop is shown.

In addition, our analysis shows that there is a direct connection between gold and geopolitics. In the short term, a 100pt increase Geopolitical Risk Index translates into 2.5% increase in the gold price. The long-term effect depends on the persistency of the event and the knock-on effects it can have on other key macro variables such as the inflation, rates, and the USD.

Chart 8: Gold now the geopolitical safe-haven of choice

Standardised (z-score) reaction to geopolitical events*

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data from 6 Jan 2015. The chart shows the standardized (rolling 200-day z-score) response of each asset to at least a 150bps daily rise in the Geopolitical Risk Index (GPR) without another occurrence within two weeks of that event. The sample is divided into pre-2022 (2015 to 2022) and post 2022 to 6 January 2026. There were 19 occurrences in total. US Tsy is the Bloomberg US Gov’t bond TR index, Gold in US$/oz.

In summary

The December rally underscored the divergent dynamics within the precious metals complex. While silver and platinum were propelled by acute physical tightness and policy distortions, gold’s rise was more a function of market spillover than a shift in its own fundamentals. As those distortions unwind, gold’s resilience – rooted in macroeconomic concerns and structural demand – is likely to reassert itself.

Looking forward, the interplay between policy risk, inflation expectations, and investor positioning will shape the path ahead. With the white metals resetting and gold’s core drivers intact, the stage is set for a recalibration of narratives – and potentially, a reversion to gold’s leadership.

Geopolitical events that reverberate globally have been a frequent feature over the last few years. While those events tend to be short-lived, partly due to the wavering focus of media, they seem frequent enough to raise the general level of investor anxiety – read risk premia. Gold has undoubtedly benefited both during the short-lived spikes and from the climate of elevated nervousness. There are few indications that the status quo will change in the foreseeable future.

Our Gold Outlook 2026 reviews how gold may perform over various macroeconomic scenarios, depending on the ultimate development of global macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions.

Footnotes

1Based on the BCOM Precious Metals Subindex Total Return as of 31 December 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
